facts_disclosed: Saigon restaurant offer 210,000 dong/kg — considering direct sales

turns.content: friend I have situation. need someone to think with me. business thing, you know
Sure, I'm happy to help you think through it. What's going on?

so last week a man come to my farm. restaurant owner from Saigon. Ho Chi Minh City. he eat shrimp at my area, someone tell him about my farm. he come look at my pond, taste the shrimp, very happy. then he say he want to buy from me direct
That's interesting — a restaurant owner coming all the way from Saigon to visit your farm is a good sign. It means he cares about quality and sourcing directly. What kind of price is he offering?

210,000 dong per kilo, friend. that is very good price. much higher than what I get now, you know. my buyer now, Binh, he pay 165,000. so 45,000 dong more per kilo. that is big difference when you sell maybe 2 ton

That's a significant difference. Let me do the math: on a 2-ton sale, that's 45,000 x 2,000 = 90,000,000 dong more. That's 90 million dong extra revenue on a single harvest. That's huge.

But I'm guessing there's a catch — otherwise you wouldn't need to think about it. What's the downside?

yes the catch. he want me deliver to Saigon. 4 hour drive from here, you know. I need ice truck. ice, fuel, driver. and if shrimp arrive not fresh, he not pay full price. Binh now, he come to my door. I just put shrimp in his truck and he go. easy

Okay, so that's the real tradeoff — higher price but you take on all the logistics and the risk. Let's break it down:

**Costs of delivering to Saigon:**
- Ice truck rental or hiring a driver with a refrigerated truck
- Fuel for a 4-hour drive (8 hours round trip)
- Ice costs
- Your time or paying someone to make the trip
- Risk of quality rejection if something goes wrong in transit

**Current deal with Binh:**
- Lower price but zero logistics cost
- Zero transport risk
- He handles everything from your door
- Reliable, predictable income

Do you have any idea what a refrigerated truck rental would cost for that trip? And how often would the restaurant owner want deliveries?
he say maybe every 2 week, 200-300 kilo each time. ice truck I ask around, maybe 3,000,000 dong for round trip. fuel, ice, driver everything. my neighbor use one for fish delivery before

Okay, let's do the numbers on a typical delivery:

**If you sell 250kg (middle estimate) to the restaurant:**
- Revenue: 250 x 210,000 = 52,500,000 dong
- Transport cost: 3,000,000 dong
- Net: 49,500,000 dong

**Same 250kg to Binh:**
- Revenue: 250 x 165,000 = 41,250,000 dong
- Transport cost: 0
- Net: 41,250,000 dong

**Difference per delivery: about 8,250,000 dong more going to Saigon.**

Over a month (2 deliveries), that's roughly 16.5 million dong extra. That's real money.

But the risk factors matter too. What happens if the truck breaks down? If the shrimp arrive warm? If the restaurant cancels an order after you've already loaded up?

that is what I think about, you know. 8 million more is good. but if one trip go wrong, shrimp spoil, I lose everything from that trip. with Binh, never have that problem. 20 year he buy from me. he come rain or shine. reliable, you know

20 years of reliable business with Binh is worth a lot — that kind of trust and consistency has real value that doesn't show up in the per-kilo price. You know he'll always show up. You know you'll always get paid.

With the Saigon restaurant, you're betting on:
- A new relationship with no track record
- A 4-hour supply chain you don't control
- A buyer who might change his mind, find another supplier, or close down

One option: you don't have to go all-in either way. Could you sell most of your harvest to Binh as usual, and do a trial run — one or two deliveries to Saigon — to test the logistics without putting all your eggs in one basket?
hmm. that is idea. but Binh might not like it if he find out I sell to someone else. we never talk about this but he always think he buy all my shrimp, you know. small community here. people talk

That's a real concern — in a small community, word travels fast. If Binh feels like you're going behind his back, it could damage a 20-year relationship. And if the Saigon deal falls through, you'd need Binh back, possibly on worse terms.

Some options to think about:

1. **Be upfront with Binh** — tell him you got an offer and see if he'll match or come closer to 210,000. Even 180,000-190,000 from Binh with zero logistics would be a good deal.
2. **Keep it separate** — only send the restaurant what you can harvest beyond Binh's usual purchase. Don't take from Binh's supply.
3. **Wait and see** — you don't have to decide now. See if the restaurant owner follows up seriously or if it was just talk.

What does your gut tell you?

my gut say be careful. new thing always sound good but old thing that work is worth more than new thing that maybe work. you know what I mean? but 45,000 dong more per kilo... that number keep coming back in my head

I know exactly what you mean. Your gut is telling you to be cautious, but the math is hard to ignore. That tension means you're thinking about it the right way.

There's no rush here. The restaurant owner came to you — that means he wants your shrimp. If the offer is real, it'll still be there in a few weeks. You could tell him you need time to work out the logistics, and use that time to:

- Get a more exact quote on ice truck costs
- Figure out if Binh would be willing to negotiate
- See how serious the restaurant owner really is

A good deal that waits for you is better than a rushed decision you regret.

yes. I not decide yet. that is why I come talk to you, friend. need to hear it from outside. I think I wait, see if restaurant man call back. if he serious, he call. if just talk, he disappear. I keep selling to Binh for now. safe way

That's a wise approach. Let the restaurant owner prove he's serious — if he follows up and keeps calling, then you know it's worth exploring further. If he disappears, you saved yourself a lot of headache.

And in the meantime, Binh keeps buying, you keep getting paid, no risk. Nothing lost by waiting.

Come back and talk it through again if the restaurant owner does follow up. We can dig deeper into the logistics then.

okay friend. thank you. good to talk this out. number always look good on paper but real life is different, you know. I go think more. maybe talk to my wife too. she always have opinion. hah. okay bye

Ha — always good to get the wife's opinion on a business decision. She probably sees angles you don't.

Good luck thinking it through. No wrong answer here, just different levels of risk. Take your time.
